abstract: This paper introduces the Paper Assistant Tool (PAT), an agentic AI framework developed at Google Research for deep scientific review of mathematics and computer science manuscripts. PAT uses a multi-stage inference-scaling pipeline—segmentation, adaptive compute budgeting, deep parallel review, and global synthesis—to detect logical, mathematical, and experimental flaws in full papers. The authors report a 34% improvement over zero-shot recall on the SPOT benchmark's math/CS proof-error subset and describe pilot deployments as a pre-submission tool at STOC 2026 and ICML 2026 covering over 4,700 papers. They also propose a four-level taxonomy of AI roles in peer review, from author-side tool to fully automated review.
key_claims: Peer review faces a scaling crisis driven by the surge of AI-assisted submissions to major conferences (e.g., combined ICLR/ICML/NeurIPS submissions estimated at ~73,883 in 2026).
Specialized inference-scaling pipelines like PAT substantially outperform single-call LLM baselines for detecting technical errors in scientific manuscripts.
AI verification tools are now mature enough to be usefully deployed as pre-submission assistants for authors (Role 1) without displacing human reviewer judgment.
A structured taxonomy of four progressive roles (Tool for Authors, Tool for Reviewers, Supporting Reviewer, Total Automation) is needed to guide policy on AI in peer review.
Automated review could enable new publication tiers (e.g., an 'AIrXiv' repository) that sit between preprints and human-reviewed venues.
methods: Design of a four-stage agentic pipeline: (1) a segmenter agent partitioning papers into thematic segments, (2) adaptive compute budgeting (Light/Medium/High thinking) per segment, (3) Deep Review agents built on Gemini Deep Think that verify each segment with full-paper context, and (4) a synthesis agent using Google Search for grounding and deduplication.
Benchmark evaluation on a filtered subset of the SPOT benchmark (26 papers, 29 equation/proof errors from math and CS retractions/errata).
Use of a logic-aware LLM autograder (audited by human authors) to judge whether generated reports capture ground-truth errors, rather than strict keyword matching.
Comparison of PAT (Gemini 3.1 Pro backbone) against zero-shot Gemini 3.1 Pro and the original SPOT SOTA.

Real-world pilot deployments at STOC 2026 (Nov 2025) and ICML 2026 (Jan 2026), providing one PAT review per submission to authors pre-deadline, followed by author surveys (n=124 STOC, n=733 ICML) and collection of qualitative testimonials.
findings: PAT achieves 89.7% detection accuracy on the SPOT math/CS proof-error subset, compared to 55.2% for zero-shot Gemini 3.1 Pro and 21.1% for the original SPOT SOTA—a 34-percentage-point gain over zero-shot.
In one case (a dual Banach spaces paper), PAT constructed a concrete counterexample invalidating a main theorem that the zero-shot baseline accepted.
Over 4,700 submissions were reviewed across the STOC and ICML pilots.
Author surveys showed 97% (STOC) and 92.1% (ICML) would use PAT again; ~90% found feedback very or mostly helpful; 55.8%/64.8% found feedback mostly or all grounded.
PAT identified substantive theory gaps requiring >1 hour to fix in 11.6% of STOC responses and 35.4% of ICML responses; 31% of ICML respondents ran new experiments as a result of PAT feedback.
Reported limitations include date hallucinations, PDF parsing issues, and occasional false claims that a valid proof is incorrect.
contributions: Introduction of PAT, a segmenter–budgeter–deep-reviewer–synthesizer agentic pipeline specialized for math/CS manuscript verification.
Empirical evidence that orchestrated inference-scaling substantially improves error detection over single-call and Pass@k baselines on a real retraction-derived benchmark.
First large-scale pilot study (STOC and ICML) of AI pre-submission review at premier CS conferences, with quantitative survey and qualitative testimonial data.
A four-level taxonomy of AI roles in peer review (Tool for Authors, Tool for Reviewers, Supporting Reviewer with a 3.5 variant for ratings, Total Automation) as a framework for policy discussion.
Proposal of adjacent concepts such as an 'AIrXiv' automated-review repository and 'AI for Students' as future directions.
framing: The paper positions itself within ongoing debates about the scalability crisis of scientific peer review under AI-accelerated paper generation, drawing on prior work documenting rising LLM usage in scientific writing and inconsistency in human review (e.g., the NeurIPS 2014/2021 experiments). It frames automated verification as a necessary counterpart to automated discovery, situating PAT alongside benchmarks like SPOT and analogizing its taxonomy to SAE vehicle autonomy levels and prior autonomy hierarchies for mathematical research.
